1:
"But I determined this with myself, that I would not come again to you in heaviness." The witnesses note Paul's resolve not to come in sorrow. Gill notes Paul, having removed the charge of inconstancy, goes on to excuse his delay and to soften the severity some thought he had used; he resolved within himself not to come in heaviness. Matthew Henry notes Paul proceeds in giving an account of his conduct. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he had determined with himself not to come again to them in heaviness; the loving resolve behind the delay — that Paul deliberately put off his visit rather than come while matters stood such that his coming must bring grief (a painful visit of rebuke), choosing to wait until they had amended, so that his coming might be joyful; not coming in heaviness. The witnesses note I determined this with myself. The witnesses note not come again to you in heaviness.

The witnesses note Paul's resolve not to come in heaviness — he delayed rather than visit while his coming must bring grief.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's resolve not to come in sorrow. The lesson the expositors draw is the loving resolve behind the delay, choosing to wait until his coming might be joyful. The witnesses note the determination: "I determined this with myself" — Gill notes Paul resolved within himself; the determination; the resolved; the with himself; the purposed; the decided. The expositors observe the excuse of delay: Paul, having removed the charge of inconstancy, excuses his delay and softens the severity some thought he used; the excuse; the delay; the severity; the softens; the charge. The witnesses press the not in heaviness: "that I would not come again to you in heaviness" — Paul would not come in heaviness (sorrow, grief); the not in heaviness; the sorrow; the grief; the painful; the avoided. The expositors draw the lesson on the loving resolve: Paul deliberately put off his visit rather than come while it must bring grief — a painful visit of rebuke; the loving resolve; the put off; the rather than grief; the painful visit; the love. The witnesses observe the waiting for amendment: Paul chose to wait until they had amended, that his coming might be joyful; the waiting; the amendment; the joyful coming; the delay; the love. The expositors note the joy desired: Paul desired his coming to be joyful, not sorrowful — hence the delay; the joy; the desired; the not sorrowful; the delay; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I determined this with myself, the not come again to you in heaviness, and the loving resolve. The verse thus records Paul's resolve not to come in sorrow — teaching the loving resolve behind the delay, choosing to wait until his coming might be joyful.
2:
"For if I make you sorry, who is he then that maketh me glad, but the same which is made sorry by me?" The witnesses note the mutual sorrow and gladness of Paul and the church. Barnes notes that if Paul came and was called to inflict sorrow by severe discipline, who would there be to give him comfort but the very persons he had grieved? The witnesses note Paul reasoning that, if he makes them sorry, the only ones to make him glad are those he has grieved; the bond of mutual feeling — that Paul's joy and the church's are so intertwined that, were he to grieve them, he would rob himself of the very gladness they alone could give, so that to wound them would be to wound his own joy; making you sorry. The witnesses note if I make you sorry. The witnesses note who... maketh me glad, but the same which is made sorry.

The witnesses note the bond of mutual feeling — if Paul grieves the church, he robs himself of the gladness they alone could give.

So the witnesses make the verse the mutual sorrow and gladness. The lesson the expositors draw is the bond of feeling — Paul's joy and the church's so intertwined that grieving them would wound his own joy. The witnesses note the making sorry: "if I make you sorry" — if Paul grieves them (by a painful visit); the making sorry; the grieve; the painful; the sorrow; the visit. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes that, were Paul called to inflict sorrow by discipline, who would comfort him but the very ones he grieved? the inflict sorrow; the discipline; the who comforts; the the grieved; the bond. The witnesses press the mutual gladness: "who is he then that maketh me glad, but the same which is made sorry by me?" — the only ones to make Paul glad are those he has grieved; the mutual gladness; the maketh me glad; the the same; the grieved; the bond. The expositors draw the lesson on the intertwined joy: Paul's joy and the church's are intertwined — grieving them robs him of the gladness they alone could give; the intertwined; the joy; the robs; the gladness; the bond. The witnesses observe the wounding of his own joy: to wound them would be to wound his own joy — for they are his joy; the wounding; the own joy; the they his joy; the bond; the love. The expositors note the love in the reasoning: the reasoning shows Paul's love — he will not grieve those who are his gladness; the love; the reasoning; the not grieve; the gladness; the affection. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if I make you sorry, the who maketh me glad but the same which is made sorry, and the mutual feeling. The verse thus records the mutual sorrow and gladness — teaching the bond of feeling, that grieving the church would wound Paul's own joy.
3:
"And I wrote this same unto you, lest, when I came, I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice; having confidence in you all, that my joy is the joy of you all." The witnesses note why Paul wrote rather than came. Gill notes Paul wrote (in his former epistle, concerning the excommunication of the incestuous man) rather than come in person, hoping to hear of their repentance, that he might not have sorrow from those who should be his joy. The witnesses note Paul explaining that he wrote rather than came, lest he should have sorrow from those who ought to be his joy, confident that his joy is theirs; the loving reason for the letter — that Paul wrote the painful letter (rather than visit) to give them opportunity to amend, so that when he came he would find joy, not grief, trusting that what gladdens him gladdens them too; I wrote this same. The witnesses note lest... I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice. The witnesses note my joy is the joy of you all.

The witnesses note why Paul wrote rather than came — to give room for amendment, that he might find joy not grief, his joy being theirs.

So the witnesses make the verse the reason for the letter. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul wrote to give room for amendment, that he might find joy not grief. The witnesses note the writing: "I wrote this same unto you" — Gill notes Paul wrote (the former letter, about the offender) rather than come; the writing; the wrote; the former letter; the rather than come; the reason. The expositors observe the purpose: "lest, when I came, I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice" — lest he have sorrow from those who should be his joy; the purpose; the lest sorrow; the of whom I ought to rejoice; the joy; the grief avoided. The witnesses press the confidence: "having confidence in you all, that my joy is the joy of you all" — Paul is confident that his joy is theirs; the confidence; the my joy is theirs; the shared joy; the trust; the bond. The expositors draw the lesson on the loving reason: Paul wrote to give them opportunity to amend, that his coming might bring joy; the loving reason; the opportunity; the amend; the joy; the love. The witnesses observe the shared joy: Paul trusts that what gladdens him gladdens them — their joy is one; the shared joy; the gladdens both; the one joy; the bond; the trust. The expositors note the hope of repentance: Paul hoped to hear of their repentance, that he might come in joy; the hope; the repentance; the come in joy; the amendment; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I wrote this same, the lest I should have sorrow, the my joy is the joy of you all. The verse thus records the reason for the letter — teaching that Paul wrote to give room for amendment, that he might find joy not grief, his joy being theirs.
4:
"For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote unto you with many tears; not that ye should be grieved, but that ye might know the love which I have more abundantly unto you." The witnesses note the tearful love behind the painful letter. Gill notes Paul wrote out of much affliction and anguish, greatly pressed in spirit and grieved at heart for the iniquities among them, which they seemed unconcerned about. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he wrote out of much affliction and anguish, with many tears, not to grieve them but to show his abundant love; the painful letter as an act of love — that Paul's severe letter was no cold rebuke but was wrung from a breaking heart and written through tears, its aim not their pain but their good, the very severity a proof of his deep affection; the tears and the love. The witnesses note out of much affliction and anguish... with many tears. The witnesses note that ye might know the love which I have... unto you.

The witnesses note the tearful love behind the painful letter — written through tears, not to grieve but to show abundant love.

So the witnesses make the verse the tearful love behind the letter. The lesson the expositors draw is that the severe letter was wrung from a loving heart, its aim not their pain but their good. The witnesses note the affliction: "out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote unto you" — Gill notes Paul wrote out of much affliction and anguish, grieved for their sins; the affliction; the anguish; the heart; the grieved; the wrote. The expositors observe the tears: "with many tears" — Paul wrote with many tears; the tears; the many; the weeping; the love; the grief. The witnesses press the not to grieve: "not that ye should be grieved" — Paul wrote not to grieve them; the not to grieve; the not their pain; the aim; the purpose; the love. The expositors draw the lesson on the aim of love: "but that ye might know the love which I have more abundantly unto you" — Paul wrote that they might know his abundant love; the aim; the love; the abundantly; the know; the affection. The witnesses observe the severity a proof of love: the very severity was a proof of love — the painful letter wrung from a breaking heart; the severity; the proof of love; the painful; the breaking heart; the affection. The expositors note the love in the rebuke: the rebuke was love — Paul grieved them only to do them good; the love; the rebuke; the their good; the grieved to heal; the affection. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the out of much affliction and anguish with many tears, the that ye might know the love, and the tearful love. The verse thus records the tearful love behind the letter — teaching that the severe letter was wrung from a loving heart, its aim not their pain but their good.
5:
"But if any have caused grief, he hath not grieved me, but in part: that I may not overcharge you all." The witnesses note the offender who grieved the church. Gill notes the incestuous person is meant, though not named — who caused much grief to himself and others; Paul takes it for granted he has caused grief. Matthew Henry draws the case of the offender. The witnesses note Paul observing that, if any has caused grief, he has grieved not Paul only but in part the whole church, yet Paul tempers the charge that he may not overburden them; the gentle handling of the offence — that the offender (the man disciplined in the first letter) grieved the whole community, not Paul alone, yet Paul moderates his words, not pressing the blame too hard upon the church, that none be overwhelmed; the one who caused grief. The witnesses note if any have caused grief. The witnesses note that I may not overcharge you all.

The witnesses note the offender who grieved the church — yet Paul tempers the charge, not pressing the blame too hard, lest any be overwhelmed.

So the witnesses make the verse the offender who grieved the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the gentle handling of the offence, Paul moderating his words lest the church be overburdened. The witnesses note the offender: "if any have caused grief" — Gill notes the incestuous person is meant, who caused grief; the offender; the caused grief; the incestuous; the not named; the man. The expositors observe the grief to all: "he hath not grieved me, but in part" — the offender grieved not Paul only, but in part the whole church; the grief to all; the not me only; the the church; the in part; the community. The witnesses press the tempering: "that I may not overcharge you all" — Paul tempers the charge, that he may not overburden them; the tempering; the not overcharge; the moderate; the not overburden; the gentle. The expositors draw the lesson on the gentle handling: Paul handles the offence gently — not pressing the blame too hard upon the church; the gentle handling; the not pressing; the blame; the church; the moderate. The witnesses observe the offence grieved the whole: the offender's sin grieved the whole community, not Paul alone — it was a public scandal; the grieved the whole; the community; the public; the scandal; the all. The expositors note the care not to overwhelm: Paul cares not to overwhelm the church with blame — tempering his words; the care; the not overwhelm; the blame; the tempered; the gentle. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if any have caused grief, the that I may not overcharge you all, and the gentle handling. The verse thus records the offender who grieved the church — teaching the gentle handling of the offence, Paul moderating his words lest the church be overburdened.
6:
"Sufficient to such a man is this punishment, which was inflicted of many." The witnesses note that the offender's punishment was sufficient. Gill notes the punishment is the excommunication or censure laid on the incestuous person by the church. Barnes notes Paul's object here is to have him restored, saying the punishment the church had inflicted was sufficient. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the punishment inflicted by the many is sufficient for such a man; the moderation of discipline — that the censure the church had laid upon the offender (his excommunication, decreed by the majority) had now done its work and was enough, so that further severity would be needless and harmful, the discipline aiming at repentance, not destruction; the punishment sufficient. The witnesses note sufficient to such a man is this punishment. The witnesses note inflicted of many.

The witnesses note the offender's punishment sufficient — the church's censure had done its work, further severity needless; discipline aims at repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse the sufficiency of the punishment. The lesson the expositors draw is the moderation of discipline — the censure had done its work and was enough, aiming at repentance not destruction. The witnesses note the punishment: "sufficient to such a man is this punishment" — Gill notes the punishment is the excommunication laid on the offender; the punishment; the excommunication; the censure; the sufficient; the enough. The expositors observe Barnes's aim: Barnes notes Paul's object is to have him restored — the punishment was sufficient; the aim; the restored; the sufficient; the restoration; the object. The witnesses press the inflicted by many: "which was inflicted of many" — the punishment was inflicted by the many (the majority of the church); the inflicted; the many; the majority; the church; the censure. The expositors draw the lesson on the moderation of discipline: the discipline had done its work and was enough — further severity would be needless and harmful; the moderation; the done its work; the enough; the further severity; the needless. The witnesses observe the aim of repentance: the discipline aimed at repentance, not destruction — and had achieved it; the aim; the repentance; the not destruction; the achieved; the discipline. The expositors note the church's act: the punishment was the church's act (the majority) — a corporate censure; the church's act; the majority; the corporate; the censure; the discipline. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sufficient to such a man is this punishment, the inflicted of many, and the sufficiency of the discipline. The verse thus records the sufficiency of the punishment — teaching the moderation of discipline, the censure having done its work, aiming at repentance not destruction.
7:
"So that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow." The witnesses note the call to forgive and comfort the penitent offender. Gill notes that, far from inflicting further punishment, they ought to forgive him by taking off the censure, which had been sufficient. Barnes notes the danger that he be overwhelmed. The witnesses note Paul urging that they ought rather to forgive and comfort the offender, lest he be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow; the restorative aim of discipline — that, the censure having worked repentance, the church must now turn from punishing to forgiving and comforting the penitent, lest excessive sorrow drive him to despair, the goal being his recovery, not his ruin; forgive and comfort him. The witnesses note ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him. The witnesses note lest... swallowed up with overmuch sorrow.

The witnesses note the call to forgive and comfort the penitent — lest he be swallowed up with sorrow; the restorative aim of discipline.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to forgive and comfort the offender. The lesson the expositors draw is the restorative aim of discipline — turning from punishing to forgiving the penitent, lest sorrow drive him to despair. The witnesses note the forgiving: "ye ought rather to forgive him" — Gill notes they ought to forgive him by taking off the censure; the forgiving; the take off the censure; the forgive; the restore; the pardon. The expositors observe the comforting: "and comfort him" — and comfort him; the comforting; the comfort; the console; the encourage; the restore. The witnesses press the danger: "lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow" — lest he be swallowed up (overwhelmed) with overmuch sorrow; the danger; the swallowed up; the overmuch sorrow; the overwhelmed; the despair. The expositors draw the lesson on the restorative aim: the censure having worked repentance, the church must turn from punishing to forgiving and comforting; the restorative; the turn from punishing; the forgive and comfort; the penitent; the recovery. The witnesses observe the danger of despair: excessive sorrow might drive the penitent to despair — hence the comfort; the despair; the excessive sorrow; the comfort; the danger; the rescue. The expositors note the goal of recovery: the goal is his recovery, not his ruin — discipline restoring, not destroying; the goal; the recovery; the not ruin; the restoring; the discipline. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye ought rather to forgive him and comfort him, the lest swallowed up with overmuch sorrow, and the restorative aim. The verse thus records the call to forgive and comfort the offender — teaching the restorative aim of discipline, turning from punishing to forgiving the penitent, lest sorrow drive him to despair.
8:
"Wherefore I beseech you that ye would confirm your love toward him." The witnesses note the exhortation to confirm love toward the offender. Gill notes Paul beseeches (exhorts) them. Barnes notes the word "confirm" means to give authority, to establish as valid — that they should give strong assurances of their love to him, pursuing such a course as to convince him of their affection. The witnesses note Paul beseeching them to confirm (ratify, publicly assure) their love toward the offender; the public restoration of love — that the church, having forgiven the penitent, must now openly and decisively renew its love to him, giving him solid assurance of acceptance, so that the forgiveness is not merely felt but formally and warmly expressed; confirm your love. The witnesses note I beseech you. The witnesses note confirm your love toward him.

The witnesses note the call to confirm love toward the offender — the church openly renewing its love, giving the penitent solid assurance of acceptance.

So the witnesses make the verse the exhortation to confirm love. The lesson the expositors draw is the public restoration of love, the church openly renewing its love to the penitent. The witnesses note the beseeching: "I beseech you" — Paul beseeches (exhorts, entreats) them; the beseeching; the exhort; the entreat; the urge; the plea. The expositors observe the confirming: "that ye would confirm your love toward him" — Barnes notes "confirm" means to establish as valid, to give strong assurances of love; the confirming; the establish; the ratify; the assure; the love. The witnesses press the public love: the church must openly and decisively renew its love to the offender — confirming it publicly; the public love; the openly; the renew; the confirm; the assurance. The expositors draw the lesson on the restoration of love: having forgiven the penitent, the church must restore its love — giving him solid assurance of acceptance; the restoration; the love; the forgiven; the assurance; the acceptance. The witnesses observe the forgiveness expressed: the forgiveness is not merely felt but formally and warmly expressed — the love confirmed; the expressed; the not merely felt; the formal; the warm; the confirmed. The expositors note the assurance of acceptance: the penitent needs solid assurance of acceptance — lest he doubt his welcome; the assurance; the acceptance; the solid; the welcome; the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I beseech you, the confirm your love toward him, and the public restoration of love. The verse thus records the exhortation to confirm love — teaching the public restoration of love, the church openly renewing its love to the penitent.
9:
"For to this end also did I write, that I might know the proof of you, whether ye be obedient in all things." The witnesses note that Paul wrote to prove their obedience. Gill notes Paul wrote, both in this and his former epistle, with this view. Barnes notes Paul does not say this was the only purpose — not that he wished arbitrarily to test them, but to prove their willingness to obey. The witnesses note Paul explaining that he wrote to know the proof of them, whether they were obedient in all things; the testing of their obedience — that one aim of the painful letter was to prove the Corinthians' submission to apostolic authority, both in disciplining the offender at first and now in forgiving him, their willingness to obey in both the severe and the gentle command being the test; the proof of obedience. The witnesses note that I might know the proof of you. The witnesses note whether ye be obedient in all things.

The witnesses note Paul wrote to prove their obedience — whether they would obey in all things, both in disciplining and in forgiving the offender.

So the witnesses make the verse the testing of their obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that one aim of the letter was to prove the Corinthians' submission, in both the severe and the gentle command. The witnesses note the end: "for to this end also did I write" — Gill notes Paul wrote with this view; the end; the wrote; the purpose; the aim; the view. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes Paul did not wish arbitrarily to test them, but to prove their willingness to obey; the not arbitrary; the willingness; the obey; the prove; the test. The witnesses press the proof: "that I might know the proof of you" — Paul wrote to know the proof (the tested quality) of them; the proof; the tested; the know; the quality; the test. The expositors draw the lesson on obedience in all things: "whether ye be obedient in all things" — the test was whether they were obedient in all things; the obedience; the all things; the test; the submission; the willingness. The witnesses observe the double obedience: their obedience was tested in both disciplining the offender at first and now forgiving him — obeying the severe and the gentle command; the double obedience; the discipline and forgiveness; the severe and gentle; the both; the submission. The expositors note the submission to authority: the test was their submission to apostolic authority — willing to obey in all; the submission; the authority; the obey; the all; the test. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that I might know the proof of you, the whether ye be obedient in all things, and the testing of obedience. The verse thus records the testing of their obedience — teaching that one aim of the letter was to prove their submission, in both the severe and the gentle command.
10:
"To whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also: for if I forgave any thing, to whom I forgave it, for your sakes forgave I it in the person of Christ;" The witnesses note Paul's joining the church in forgiving the offender. Gill notes this may extend to the forgiveness of any person in any case, though it has particular respect to the incestuous man, whose forgiveness Paul was ready to join with the church in. The witnesses note Paul declaring that whom they forgive, he forgives also, and that what he forgave, he forgave for their sakes in the person (presence, or stead) of Christ; the unity of apostle and church in forgiveness — that Paul joins his pardon to theirs, exercising his forgiveness not for himself but for their good, and doing so "in the person of Christ" (as Christ's representative, by his authority and in his presence), so that the church's forgiveness carries Christ's own warrant; I forgive also. The witnesses note to whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also. The witnesses note for your sakes... in the person of Christ.

The witnesses note Paul joining the church in forgiving — his pardon added to theirs, granted for their sakes in the person of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's joining the church in forgiveness. The lesson the expositors draw is the unity of apostle and church in pardon, Paul forgiving for their good in Christ's person. The witnesses note the joining: "to whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also" — Gill notes Paul joins the church in forgiving the offender; the joining; the I forgive also; the with the church; the pardon; the unity. The expositors observe the for their sakes: "for if I forgave any thing... for your sakes forgave I it" — Paul forgave for their sakes (their good), not his own; the for your sakes; the their good; the not his own; the forgave; the love. The witnesses press the person of Christ: "in the person of Christ" — Paul forgave in the person (presence, or stead) of Christ; the person of Christ; the representative; the stead; the presence; the authority. The expositors draw the lesson on the unity of pardon: Paul joins his pardon to the church's — apostle and church united in forgiveness; the unity; the pardon; the apostle and church; the joined; the forgiveness. The witnesses observe the forgiveness in Christ's authority: Paul forgives "in the person of Christ" — as Christ's representative, by his authority and in his presence; the in Christ's authority; the representative; the by his authority; the presence; the warrant. The expositors note the church's pardon warranted: the church's forgiveness, joined by Paul in Christ's person, carries Christ's own warrant; the warrant; the church's pardon; the Christ's; the joined; the authority. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to whom ye forgive I forgive also, the for your sakes in the person of Christ, and the unity of pardon. The verse thus records Paul's joining the church in forgiveness — teaching the unity of apostle and church in pardon, Paul forgiving for their good in Christ's person.
11:
"Lest Satan should get an advantage of us: for we are not ignorant of his devices." The witnesses note the danger of Satan's advantage through unforgiveness. Gill notes Satan gets an advantage of the churches when discipline is brought into neglect or turned into tyranny, or when he can draw off any person from the church; the metaphor is of a covetous trader circumventing others. The witnesses note Paul giving the reason for forgiving — lest Satan get an advantage of them, for they are not ignorant of his devices; the vigilance against the enemy — that excessive severity (refusing to restore the penitent) would play into Satan's hand, who would use it to drive the man to despair or to harden the church, so that the very discipline meant to defeat sin could, if pushed too far, become Satan's tool; Satan's devices. The witnesses note lest Satan should get an advantage. The witnesses note not ignorant of his devices.

The witnesses note the danger of Satan's advantage — excessive severity would play into his hand; the believers not ignorant of his devices.

So the witnesses make the verse the danger of Satan's advantage through unforgiveness. The lesson the expositors draw is that excessive severity would play into Satan's hand, against which the believers must be vigilant. The witnesses note the advantage: "lest Satan should get an advantage of us" — Gill notes Satan gets an advantage when discipline is neglected or turned into tyranny; the advantage; the Satan; the discipline; the tyranny; the danger. The expositors observe the device: "for we are not ignorant of his devices" — the believers are not ignorant of Satan's devices (schemes, wiles); the devices; the schemes; the wiles; the not ignorant; the vigilance. The witnesses press the danger of severity: excessive severity (refusing to restore the penitent) would play into Satan's hand — driving the man to despair or hardening the church; the danger; the severity; the despair; the hardening; the Satan's hand. The expositors draw the lesson on discipline becoming Satan's tool: the very discipline meant to defeat sin could, if pushed too far, become Satan's tool — driving to despair; the discipline; the Satan's tool; the pushed too far; the despair; the danger. The witnesses observe the vigilance: the believers must be vigilant against Satan — not ignorant of his devices; the vigilance; the against Satan; the not ignorant; the devices; the watchful. The expositors note the wisdom of moderation: the wisdom of moderation (forgiving the penitent) defeats Satan's scheme; the wisdom; the moderation; the forgiving; the defeats Satan; the scheme. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the lest Satan should get an advantage, the not ignorant of his devices, and the danger of severity. The verse thus records the danger of Satan's advantage — teaching that excessive severity would play into his hand, against which the believers must be vigilant.
12:
"Furthermore, when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, and a door was opened unto me of the Lord," The witnesses note the open door at Troas. Chrysostom notes these words seem unworthy of Paul if, for a brother's absence, he threw away so great an opportunity of saving; yet on examination they hang together and are worthy of him. The witnesses note Paul recounting that, coming to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, a door was opened to him by the Lord; the providential opportunity — that at Troas Paul found a wide and effectual opening for the gospel (a "door opened of the Lord"), a ripe field for the saving of souls, granted by divine providence, which made all the more striking the restlessness that followed; the open door at Troas. The witnesses note when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel. The witnesses note a door was opened unto me of the Lord.

The witnesses note the open door at Troas — a wide and effectual opening for the gospel, granted by the Lord; a ripe field for souls.

So the witnesses make the verse the open door at Troas. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential opportunity, a wide and effectual opening for the gospel granted by the Lord. The witnesses note the coming: "when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel" — Paul came to Troas to preach the gospel; the coming; the Troas; the preach; the gospel; the mission. The expositors observe the open door: "a door was opened unto me of the Lord" — a door was opened to Paul by the Lord (a wide, effectual opening); the open door; the opened; the by the Lord; the opening; the opportunity. The witnesses press the providential opportunity: the open door was a providential opportunity — a ripe field for the saving of souls; the providential; the opportunity; the ripe field; the souls; the opening. The expositors draw the lesson on the door of the Lord: the door was opened "of the Lord" — by divine providence, not chance; the door of the Lord; the providence; the not chance; the opened; the divine. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the seeming unworthiness (Paul leaving so great an opportunity for a brother's absence), yet it hangs together; the seeming unworthy; the opportunity; the brother's absence; the hangs together; the worthy. The expositors note the contrast with the restlessness: the open door makes all the more striking the restlessness that follows (v.13) — that Paul left even a ripe field; the contrast; the restlessness; the striking; the left the field; the v.13. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, the door was opened unto me of the Lord, and the open door. The verse thus records the open door at Troas — teaching the providential opportunity, a wide and effectual opening for the gospel granted by the Lord.
13:
"I had no rest in my spirit, because I found not Titus my brother: but taking my leave of them, I went from thence into Macedonia." The witnesses note Paul's restlessness for lack of Titus. Gill notes that, though such a door was opened, Paul was greatly distressed in mind, restless and uneasy, and could not be satisfied to stay — which shows that, though so great a man, he was but a man, of like passions with others. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he had no rest in his spirit because he found not Titus, and so left Troas for Macedonia; the human anxiety of the apostle — that Paul's concern for the Corinthians (whose news he awaited by Titus) so weighed on him that even an open door could not hold him, and his restless love drove him on to Macedonia to seek tidings, showing the apostle a man of deep affection and real feeling; no rest in my spirit. The witnesses note I had no rest in my spirit. The witnesses note I found not Titus my brother.

The witnesses note Paul's restlessness for lack of Titus — his anxious love for the Corinthians driving him from even an open door to Macedonia.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's restlessness for lack of Titus. The lesson the expositors draw is the human anxiety of the apostle, his love for the Corinthians driving him from even an open door. The witnesses note the no rest: "I had no rest in my spirit" — Gill notes Paul was distressed, restless, and uneasy; the no rest; the distressed; the restless; the uneasy; the spirit. The expositors observe the cause: "because I found not Titus my brother" — Paul found not Titus (who was to bring news of Corinth); the cause; the not Titus; the the news; the awaited; the anxiety. The witnesses press the leaving: "taking my leave of them, I went from thence into Macedonia" — Paul left Troas for Macedonia; the leaving; the from Troas; the to Macedonia; the went; the departed. The expositors draw the lesson on the human anxiety: Paul's concern for the Corinthians so weighed on him that even an open door could not hold him; the human anxiety; the concern; the weighed; the open door; the could not hold. The witnesses observe the restless love: Paul's restless love drove him to Macedonia to seek tidings — his affection for the Corinthians; the restless love; the to Macedonia; the seek tidings; the affection; the the Corinthians. The expositors note the apostle a man of feeling: Gill notes this shows the apostle a man of like passions — of deep affection and real feeling; the man of feeling; the like passions; the affection; the real feeling; the human. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I had no rest in my spirit, the I found not Titus my brother, and the restless love. The verse thus records Paul's restlessness for lack of Titus — teaching the human anxiety of the apostle, his love for the Corinthians driving him from even an open door.
14:
"Now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ, and maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place." The witnesses note the thanksgiving for the triumph in Christ. Gill notes Paul, recalling the door opened at Troas and the manifold appearances of God blessing their labours to the conversion of souls, breaks forth into thanksgiving. The witnesses note Paul giving thanks to God, who always causes them to triumph in Christ and makes manifest the savour of his knowledge in every place; the triumphal procession of the gospel — that God, like a victorious general leading a triumph, leads his apostles in Christ's train, spreading everywhere through them the fragrant knowledge of Christ, so that the very ministry that brought Paul affliction is a continual victory and a diffusing of the sweet aroma of the gospel; the triumph in Christ. The witnesses note always causeth us to triumph in Christ. The witnesses note maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge.

The witnesses note the thanksgiving for the triumph in Christ — God leading his apostles in Christ's triumphal train, spreading the fragrance of his knowledge.

So the witnesses make the verse the thanksgiving for the triumph in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the triumphal procession of the gospel, God leading his apostles in Christ's train and spreading the knowledge of Christ. The witnesses note the thanks: "thanks be unto God" — Gill notes Paul breaks forth into thanksgiving for God's blessing on their labours; the thanks; the thanksgiving; the to God; the blessing; the praise. The expositors observe the triumph: "which always causeth us to triumph in Christ" — God always causes them to triumph in Christ; the triumph; the always; the in Christ; the victory; the led in triumph. The witnesses press the triumphal procession: the image is of a victorious general leading a triumph — God leading his apostles in Christ's train; the triumphal procession; the general; the triumph; the train; the led. The expositors draw the lesson on the savour spread: "maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place" — God spreads through them the fragrant knowledge of Christ everywhere; the savour; the knowledge; the fragrance; the every place; the spread. The witnesses observe the ministry a victory: the very ministry that brought Paul affliction is a continual victory — a triumph in Christ; the victory; the ministry; the affliction; the triumph; the continual. The expositors note the fragrance of the gospel: the gospel diffuses the sweet aroma of the knowledge of Christ — a fragrance everywhere; the fragrance; the aroma; the knowledge; the everywhere; the diffused. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the always causeth us to triumph in Christ, the maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge, and the triumph in Christ. The verse thus records the thanksgiving for the triumph in Christ — teaching the triumphal procession of the gospel, God leading his apostles in Christ's train and spreading the knowledge of Christ.
15:
"For we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, in them that are saved, and in them that perish:" The witnesses note the apostles as a sweet savour of Christ to God. Gill notes the ministers are a sweet savour, not in themselves (for they have the same corrupt hearts as others), but as having the gifts and graces of the Spirit, comparable to fragrant spices, and as preaching Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, both in those who are saved and those who perish; the acceptableness of faithful ministry to God — that the apostles' faithful preaching of Christ is a fragrant offering well-pleasing to God, regardless of how men receive it, the same gospel ascending as a sweet aroma whether its hearers are saved by it or perish in rejecting it; a sweet savour of Christ. The witnesses note we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ. The witnesses note in them that are saved, and in them that perish.

The witnesses note the apostles a sweet savour of Christ to God — their faithful preaching well-pleasing to God whether hearers are saved or perish.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles as a sweet savour to God. The lesson the expositors draw is the acceptableness of faithful ministry to God, regardless of how men receive it. The witnesses note the sweet savour: "we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ" — Gill notes the ministers are a sweet savour, not in themselves but as preaching Christ; the sweet savour; the of Christ; the fragrant; the preaching; the offering. The expositors observe the to God: the savour is "unto God" — well-pleasing to God, an acceptable offering; the to God; the well-pleasing; the acceptable; the offering; the fragrant. The witnesses press the both classes: "in them that are saved, and in them that perish" — the savour is a sweet savour both in those saved and those who perish; the both classes; the saved; the perish; the both; the regardless. The expositors draw the lesson on the acceptableness to God: the faithful preaching of Christ is well-pleasing to God, regardless of how men receive it; the acceptableness; the faithful preaching; the well-pleasing; the regardless; the to God. The witnesses observe the same gospel: the same gospel ascends as a sweet aroma — whether hearers are saved by it or perish rejecting it; the same gospel; the sweet aroma; the saved or perish; the ascends; the fragrant. The expositors note the minister's acceptance: the faithful minister is accepted of God — his savour sweet, whatever the outcome among men; the acceptance; the faithful minister; the sweet; the whatever the outcome; the God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, the in them that are saved and them that perish, and the acceptableness to God. The verse thus records the apostles as a sweet savour to God — teaching the acceptableness of faithful ministry to God, regardless of how men receive it.
16:
"To the one we are the savour of death unto death; and to the other the savour of life unto life. And who is sufficient for these things?" The witnesses note the twofold effect of the gospel and the cry of insufficiency. Gill notes that to the one (those appointed to death) the apostles are the savour of death unto death; what Paul says of the gospel, the Jews said of the law. The witnesses note Paul declaring that to the one they are the savour of death unto death, to the other the savour of life unto life, and asking who is sufficient for these things; the solemn double effect of the gospel — that the same preached Christ becomes, to those who reject him, a deepening condemnation (death unto death), and to those who believe, an ever-increasing life (life unto life), an outcome so weighty that Paul cries out at the awful responsibility, "who is sufficient?"; the savour of death and life. The witnesses note the savour of death unto death... the savour of life unto life. The witnesses note who is sufficient for these things?

The witnesses note the twofold effect — the same gospel a savour of death to those who perish, of life to the saved — and Paul's cry, "who is sufficient?"

So the witnesses make the verse the twofold effect of the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the solemn double outcome of the same preached Christ, and the awful responsibility that draws Paul's cry. The witnesses note the death: "to the one we are the savour of death unto death" — Gill notes to those appointed to death, the apostles are the savour of death unto death; the death; the savour of death; the unto death; the condemnation; the perish. The expositors observe the life: "to the other the savour of life unto life" — to the other (the saved), they are the savour of life unto life; the life; the savour of life; the unto life; the salvation; the saved. The witnesses press the double effect: the same preached Christ becomes a deepening condemnation to those who reject him and an increasing life to those who believe; the double effect; the same Christ; the condemnation and life; the reject and believe; the outcome. The expositors draw the lesson on the solemn outcome: the outcome is solemn — death unto death, or life unto life; the same gospel dividing; the solemn; the outcome; the dividing; the weighty. The witnesses observe the cry of insufficiency: "who is sufficient for these things?" — Paul cries out at the awful responsibility — who is sufficient? the cry; the insufficiency; the responsibility; the awful; the who is sufficient. The expositors note the weight of the ministry: the weight of the ministry (with such eternal outcomes) draws the cry — none sufficient in himself; the weight; the ministry; the eternal outcomes; the cry; the none sufficient. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the savour of death unto death and life unto life, the who is sufficient for these things, and the twofold effect. The verse thus records the twofold effect of the gospel — teaching the solemn double outcome of the same preached Christ, and the awful responsibility that draws Paul's cry.
17:
"For we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God: but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ." The witnesses note the contrast of Paul's sincerity with the corrupters of the word. Gill notes Paul removes from himself a character belonging not to him but to the false apostles, who are described as "many" — great swarms of false teachers in the early times of Christianity. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they are not as the many who corrupt the word of God, but speak of sincerity, as of God, in God's sight, in Christ; the integrity of the true minister — that, unlike the many false teachers who adulterate God's word (as a trader corrupts his goods for gain), Paul preaches with pure sincerity, from God as his source, as in God's presence, and in union with Christ, his ministry marked by transparent honesty before the all-seeing God; not as many which corrupt the word. The witnesses note not as many, which corrupt the word of God. The witnesses note as of sincerity... in the sight of God speak we in Christ.

The witnesses note the contrast — Paul not as the many who corrupt the word, but speaking with sincerity, as of God, in God's sight, in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the contrast of Paul's sincerity with the corrupters. The lesson the expositors draw is the integrity of the true minister, who preaches with pure sincerity in God's sight, against those who adulterate the word. The witnesses note the corrupters: "we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God" — Gill notes the "many" are the false apostles who corrupt God's word; the corrupters; the many; the false apostles; the corrupt; the adulterate. The expositors observe the adulterating: the false teachers adulterate God's word — as a trader corrupts his goods for gain; the adulterating; the corrupt; the for gain; the goods; the trader. The witnesses press the sincerity: "but as of sincerity" — Paul speaks of sincerity (purity, single-heartedness); the sincerity; the purity; the single-hearted; the honest; the genuine. The expositors draw the lesson on as of God: "but as of God, in the sight of God" — Paul speaks as of God (God his source) and in God's sight (presence); the as of God; the source; the in God's sight; the presence; the all-seeing. The witnesses observe the in Christ: "speak we in Christ" — Paul speaks in Christ (in union with him, by his authority); the in Christ; the union; the authority; the speak; the ministry. The expositors note the integrity before God: the true minister preaches with transparent honesty before the all-seeing God — no corruption, no gain-seeking; the integrity; the transparent; the before God; the no corruption; the honesty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not as many which corrupt the word of God, the in the sight of God speak we in Christ, and the integrity of the true minister. The verse thus records the contrast of Paul's sincerity with the corrupters — teaching the integrity of the true minister, who preaches with pure sincerity in God's sight, against those who adulterate the word.
